title: Windows Situational Awareness
description: Perform internal enumeration on a Windows target (optional lab).
problemStatement:
# Objective
Practice situational awareness commands on a Windows system. (If you have a Windows VM in your lab, use it; otherwise, you can run these on your Kali host using PowerShell Core or review the commands).

## Commands to Run and Document Output
Run the following commands and note what information each provides:

1.  `whoami /all`
2.  `systeminfo | findstr /B /C:"OS Name" /C:"OS Version" /C:"System Type"`
3.  `ipconfig /all`
4.  `netstat -ano | findstr LISTENING`
5.  `tasklist /svc`
6.  `net user`
7.  `net localgroup Administrators`
8.  `cmdkey /list`

## Deliverable
A brief summary of the key findings from each command (e.g., "User is 'bob', has SeImpersonatePrivilege. System is Windows 10 Pro 19045. Listening ports: 445, 3389. Cached credentials found for TERMSRV/192.168.1.100.")
hints: Use `whoami /priv` to check for dangerous privileges like SeImpersonatePrivilege.
`cmdkey /list` shows stored credentials for network resources.
expectedOutput: A concise summary of the system's security posture.
solutionCode:
# Example summary:
# User: CORP\jsmith (Standard User)
# Privileges: SeImpersonatePrivilege (Enabled) -> Potato attack possible.
# OS: Windows 10 Enterprise 22H2
# Listening Ports: 445 (SMB), 3389 (RDP)
# Running Services: MSSQLSERVER, WinRM
# Local Admins: Administrator, CORP\Domain Admins
# Cached Creds: TERMSRV/dc01.corp.local
# Overall: Host is domain-joined. Potential RDP lateral movement target. MSSQL service may have stored credentials.
